symbols: Peter's key (implied) — the authority to bind and loose given by Christ (Matthew 16:19)
Paul's open book — the Epistles through which the Gospel was transmitted to the Gentile world
the two figures together — the twin pillars of the Church, Jew and Gentile united in Christ
Peter's golden cloak — the warmth of pastoral leadership, the shepherd of Christ's flock
Paul's red mantle — the fire of apostolic zeal and eventual martyrdom
description: Two bearded men stand side by side in a half-length portrait against a dark background. The man on the left has gray curly hair and beard, wearing a golden-ochre cloak over a white garment, his hands clasped together at waist level. The man on the right is balding with a dark pointed beard, wearing a deep red mantle over a white shirt, his right hand resting on an open book on a ledge below. Both gaze directly at the viewer with intense, penetrating expressions. The dramatic lighting illuminates their faces and hands from the left.
theological_description: El Greco pairs the two pillars of the apostolic Church — Peter and Paul — whose partnership and occasional tension defined early Christianity. Peter, the first called, received from Christ the keys of the kingdom (Matthew 16:18-19) and became the shepherd of the Jewish Christian community. Paul, the former persecutor transformed on the Damascus road (Acts 9:1-19), became the apostle to the Gentiles (Galatians 2:7-8). Paul himself describes the decisive meeting at the Jerusalem Council where they 'gave the right hand of fellowship' to him and Barnabas (Galatians 2:9), recognizing that the same God who worked through Peter for the circumcised worked through Paul for the uncircumcised. Paul's open book signifies the Epistles — the theological foundation of Christian doctrine. Together they represent the universality of the Gospel: to the Jew first, and also to the Greek (Romans 1:16). Tradition holds both were martyred in Rome under Nero, their blood seeding the Church they built.

scripture_refs.reason: Christ gives Peter the keys to the kingdom and authority to bind and loose
Peter entrusted with the Gospel to the circumcised, Paul to the uncircumcised — their complementary missions
Paul's conversion on the Damascus road — the foundation of his apostleship
The Gospel as power of salvation to the Jew first and also to the Greek — embodied in this pairing
